content.text: Platform Friction Tracking and Backlog Prioritization

A Claude platform team that ships improvements without a structured friction-tracking system often fixes the wrong things: the noisiest complaints rather than the highest-impact ones, or one-time incidents rather than systemic patterns. Platform friction tracking is the discipline of
developer pain points so that each sprint's platform investment goes to the frictions that most reliably predict developer experience outcomes like TTFD, NPS, and SDK adoption rate.

attrs.alt: Friction tracking pipeline: five signal sources → weekly triage → priority scoring → platform sprint

content.text: Friction arrives through five distinct channels, each with different temporal characteristics and different populations of reporters. A complete friction-tracking system draws from all five — no single channel is sufficient.
Underreports — most engineers don't file entries
Delayed — 3 months between signal and analysis
Biased toward hard-to-find issues; easy issues self-resolve
The friction log is the primary continuous signal. Every entry must capture enough context for the platform team to reproduce and prioritise the issue without a follow-up conversation.

<!-- friction-log-entry template -->
## [AREA] Short description of the friction

**Date:** 2025-04-08
**Team:** search-ranking
**Area:** SDK / Auth / Docs / CI / Provisioning / Cost / Other
**Severity:** 1 (minor inconvenience) | 2 (cost me hours) | 3 (blocked me or my team)
**Description:**
<!-- What were you trying to do? What happened instead? -->
Tried to run `acme-claude-sdk` against our staging environment with a
read-only API key. Got a 403 with no actionable error message — just
"Forbidden". Spent 2 hours before realising that read-only keys can't
call the Messages API.

**Workaround found:** Yes — switched to a full-access key.
**Affected others on my team:** Yes (2 engineers hit same issue)

Support tickets arrive individually but cluster into themes when reviewed in aggregate. The platform team runs a theme clustering pass every Friday on the previous week's tickets, tagging each with a primary area label and a friction sub-type.

# scripts/cluster_tickets.py  — weekly ticket theme analysis
from collections import Counter
import json

# Tickets exported from the internal support tool
with open("data/tickets_this_week.json") as f:
    tickets = json.load(f)

# Manual area labels assigned during weekly review
AREA_LABELS = [
    "provisioning", "auth", "sdk-api", "sdk-docs",
    "ci-cd", "cost-visibility", "eval-runner", "other"
]

area_counts = Counter(t["area_label"] for t in tickets)
severity_by_area = {}
for area in AREA_LABELS:
    area_tickets = [t for t in tickets if t["area_label"] == area]
    if area_tickets:
        avg_severity = sum(t["severity"] for t in area_tickets) / len(area_tickets)
        severity_by_area[area] = round(avg_severity, 2)

print("Ticket themes this week:")
for area, count in area_counts.most_common():
    sev = severity_by_area.get(area, 0)
    print(f"  {area}: {count} tickets, avg severity {sev}")

# Output example:
# auth: 7 tickets, avg severity 2.4
# sdk-docs: 5 tickets, avg severity 1.8
# provisioning: 2 tickets, avg severity 1.0
Once friction items are collected and clustered, the platform team scores each one to determine its position in the backlog. The scoring model uses three factors:
(how severely it affects TTFD or NPS), and
(engineering days to fix). Priority Score = (Reach × Impact) ÷ Effort.

Platform friction work competes with feature development and maintenance for sprint capacity. Without an explicit allocation rule, friction items are perpetually deferred in favour of roadmap features — a common failure mode that degrades NPS quarter over quarter. The
: the platform team reserves 20% of each sprint's capacity for P0 and P1 friction items. This is a standing commitment, not a per-sprint decision.

The 20% rule is enforced by the Product Owner, not by the engineers. Engineers cannot voluntarily give up the 20% allocation to hit a roadmap deadline — doing so requires PO approval and an explicit plan to address the deferred frictions in the following sprint.

## Sprint Planning Checklist — Friction Allocation

**Before accepting roadmap items for this sprint:**
1. List all P0 friction items in the backlog. Each MUST be in this sprint.
2. List all P1 friction items. Fill the 20% capacity with P1 items.
3. If P0 items alone exceed 20%, this sprint is a friction-focused sprint.
   Roadmap items are deferred. Escalate to PO.

**Capacity calculation:**
- Total sprint capacity: 40 engineer-days
- Friction allocation (20%): 8 engineer-days
- Roadmap capacity: 32 engineer-days

**Sprint friction items:**
| Item | Area | Priority Score | Effort | Owner |
|------|------|----------------|--------|-------|
| F-101: Fix 403 error message for read-only keys | auth | 76.8 (P0) | 0.5d | @alice |
| F-87: Add streaming example to SDK docs | sdk-docs | 18.0 (P1) | 1.0d | @bob |
| F-95: Document ACME_PROJECT_ID env var | docs | 12.0 (P1) | 0.5d | @carol |
| Total | | | 2.0d | |
Remaining friction capacity (6d) → carry to next sprint's P1 pool.
Closing the Loop on Friction Items
Every friction item resolved in a sprint must be closed in two places: the backlog (marked resolved with a link to the fix) and the
, which is published monthly to the platform Slack channel. The report shows what was fixed, what is in progress, and what is intentionally deferred (and why).

## Platform Friction Report — April 2025

### Fixed This Month (8 items)
| Item | Area | Fix | Impact |
|------|------|-----|--------|
| F-101: Cryptic 403 for read-only keys | auth | Error message now says "Read-only keys cannot call Messages API — request a full-access key at IDP." | Ticket vol ↓ 4/week |
| F-87: No streaming example | sdk-docs | Added streaming_response.py to examples/ | Docs CSAT +0.3 |
| F-78: No cost visibility by team | cost | Per-team spend dashboard live in IDP | Survey theme resolved |

### In Progress (3 items)
- F-94: eval-runner Python 3.11 requirement (P2 → blocked on upstream dep)
- F-99: Copier template doesn't set ACME_TEAM_ID automatically (P1, 60% done)

### Won't Fix (2 items, with rationale)
- F-66: Support GPT-4o through acme-claude-sdk — out of scope; violates standardisation goal
- F-52: Auto-generate PR descriptions using Claude — engineering team scope, not platform scope

### Next Month's P0 / P1 Pool
[4 items scored and ready for sprint assignment]

The priority scoring model uses TTFD impact as one factor, but friction items should also be linked to the quarterly DevEx survey themes and the NPS driver analysis. This cross-linking reveals whether a single friction item is suppressing multiple metrics simultaneously — which increases its effective priority score.

A friction backlog that is never pruned accumulates stale items and loses credibility. Engineers stop filing new entries when they see that old ones have been sitting unresolved for 6+ months. The platform team performs a
pass to age out, reclassify, or formally decline items that are no longer relevant.
Items more than 6 months old with no new mentions are moved to 'Archived — no recent signal'. They can be reopened if the issue resurfaces.
Items that have been superseded by a platform change (e.g. a new SDK version eliminates the pain point) are closed with a link to the change.
Items re-scored quarterly: if a new platform release has changed the effort estimate, the priority score is recalculated.
Items that are out of scope for the platform team are formally declined with a rationale and a pointer to the team that owns the relevant area.

# scripts/backlog_prune.py  — run quarterly before sprint planning
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import json

with open("data/friction_backlog.json") as f:
    items = json.load(f)

cutoff = datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(days=180)
archived, active = [], []

for item in items:
    last_mention = datetime.fromisoformat(item["last_mention_at"])
    if last_mention < cutoff and item["mention_count"] < 3:
        item["status"] = "archived"
        item["archive_reason"] = "No new signal in 6 months"
        archived.append(item)
    else:
        active.append(item)

print(f"Archived: {len(archived)} items")
print(f"Active: {len(active)} items")

with open("data/friction_backlog.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(active, f, indent=2)
with open("data/friction_backlog_archived.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(archived, f, indent=2)

Consistent area labelling is what makes aggregate analysis possible. Without a shared taxonomy, 'auth issues' in one week's triage become 'security problems' in the next, and trend analysis becomes impossible. The platform team maintains a fixed taxonomy with at most eight top-level areas.
API key creation, quota management, workspace setup
SDK authentication, token refresh, permission errors
SDK function signatures, error types, return values
complete() raises AttributeError on empty string
SDK README, docstrings, examples, migration guides
CI templates, eval gate, CD pipeline integration
Per-team spend, quota dashboards, billing alerts
No way to see my team's monthly spend
Internal developer portal UI, service catalog, runbooks
Can't find the quota increase form in IDP
Anything that doesn't fit the above
Cross-team pattern requiring new area label
Friction log as a complaint box:
entries without severity, area, or workaround information cannot be triaged. Enforce the template; don't accept free-form rants.
the engineer who posts in Slack most frequently about an issue is not necessarily the one experiencing the highest-impact friction. Always score objectively with Reach × Impact ÷ Effort.
Deferring P0 items for roadmap features:
this is the most common failure mode. The 20% rule exists precisely to prevent this. Track deferred P0 items explicitly and report to the PO.
mark an item closed only after the fix has been deployed and at least one affected team confirms the friction is gone. 'Fix shipped' is not 'issue resolved'.
every item that the platform team decides not to address must have a documented rationale. Undocumented rejections erode trust and generate repeat filings of the same issue.

Friction tracking does not require dedicated software. The platform team starts with the simplest tool that allows all five signals to funnel into one place, and graduates to more structured tooling only when the volume of items warrants it.
Shared Markdown file in the IDP wiki
No search, scoring requires manual calculation
GitHub Issues with area labels and priority score field
Linear or Jira with custom friction board
Overhead of maintaining a second backlog
Custom dashboard fed by telemetry + ticket export
Platform team > 5 engineers, dedicated DevEx function
Engineering investment to build and maintain

Start with GitHub Issues and the area label taxonomy. Add the priority score as a custom field. This handles the first 18 months of a platform team's friction tracking needs without any custom tooling investment.

Platform friction tracking converts scattered developer complaints into a prioritised, measurable backlog that the platform team can act on systematically. The five signal channels — friction log, DevEx survey, support tickets, telemetry anomalies, and office hours — each surface different friction classes. Weekly triage clusters them by area and severity. The
priority score model ranks items objectively. The
ensures friction items are addressed continuously rather than deferred indefinitely. Closing the loop with a monthly friction report maintains trust that the system is working.
Collect friction from all five channels — no single source is complete.
Score each item with Reach × Impact ÷ Effort to rank the backlog objectively.
P0 items (score ≥ 20) must be in the current sprint; P1 items fill the 20% allocation.
The 20% sprint allocation is a standing commitment enforced by the Product Owner.
Publish a monthly friction report listing fixed, in-progress, and won't-fix items with rationale.
Cross-link friction items to TTFD sub-metrics and survey themes — items that suppress multiple metrics have higher effective priority.
